report_text: For the 2007 shock, multi-indicator validation was computed across 6 available macro outcome metrics. Frontier countries show more favourable outcomes than non-frontier countries on 5 metrics, while non-frontier countries perform better on 1 metrics. This evidence is descriptive and does not imply causality.

For the 2019 shock, multi-indicator validation was computed across 6 available macro outcome metrics. Frontier countries show more favourable outcomes than non-frontier countries on 5 metrics, while non-frontier countries perform better on 1 metrics. This evidence is descriptive and does not imply causality.

The multi-indicator validation step evaluates post-shock macro outcomes after the POSet has already been constructed from pre-shock structural variables. This prevents leakage from validation outcomes into the ordering relation.

The validation indicators include GDP growth, unemployment, inflation stability, public debt, and productivity. They are selected because they describe different dimensions of post-shock macroeconomic performance rather than the same structural capacities used for ordering.

Mismatch cases are expected in a multidimensional resilience framework. A frontier country may underperform on a particular outcome, or a deeper-layer country may perform strongly, because recovery also depends on shock exposure, policy response, sectoral composition, and factors outside the selected structural variables.
